:mod:`factory` --- Factory class
================================

.. module:: core.factory
   :synopsis: Factory utility with a couple of goodies.

.. versionadded:: 0.1
.. moduleauthor:: Claude Gibert <claude.gibert@synopticview.co.uk>

The Factory class
-----------------

.. class:: Factory(dict)

    The **Factory** class registers functions and classes with an associated identifier
    either a string or anything hashable.

    .. method:: Factory(noduplicates = True, hasdefault = True)

    	:param noduplicates: if True, a IndexError is raised if the user attempts to register the same identifier twice. Otherwise the second register overrides the first one.
    	:param hasdefault: if True, when an identifier is not found by the default functions, the special string '__default__' is searched.  If it has been registered, then any unknown identifier will trigger the creation of what was registered under the '__default__' keyword.

    .. method:: register(name,what)

    	:param name: the identifier used to later instanciate an object
    	:type name: a hashable object
    	:param what: a class or a callable object

    	Registers a callable object under the name *name*. Any hashable object
    	can be used for *name*. *What* is a callable object.

    .. method:: create(what, \*args, \*\*kargs)

    	Finds the entry under the name *what* and calls the object stored there with
    	the arguments \*args, \*\*kargs. Whatever is returned by the callable object is
    	returned by this function. If there is no default, and what is not found,
    	an IndexError is raised.

    .. method:: get(what):

    	This is identical to :func:`create` except that it does not instanciate
        classes or call functions, it just returns the callable which was registered
        in the factory.

.. class:: SingletonFactory(Factory)

    Inherits the behaviour from Factory. The difference is that when create is
    called for an identifier, the result is cached. Whenever it is called again 
    with the same identifier, the cache is returned. This results in creating
    singletons if a class is registered.

An example::

    from semperpy.core.factory import Factory, SingletonFactory

    class IntWrapper(object):

    	def __init__(self,value):
    		self.value = value

    	def __str__(self):
    		return str(self.value)

    	def increment(self):
    		self.value += 1

    def no(*args,**kargs):
    	raise IndexError('This is the default function')


    f = Factory()
    f.register('int',IntWrapper)
    f.register('__default__',no)

    i = f.create('int',2)
    print i
    2

    s = SingletonFactory()
    s.register('int',IntWrapper)
    i = s.create('int',2)
    print i
    2
    i.increment()
    print i
    3
    j = s.create('int',2)
    print j
    3

    f.create('unknown')
    Traceback (most recent call last):
    IndexError: Factory does not know how to create "unknown"
